A reader is given two kinds of material.

First, there is a theoretical dispute about systems that seem to become intelligent or insightful after enough processing. One side of the dispute rejects a sharp “critical threshold.” If novelty seems to appear, that appearance may instead be a smooth rise in deceptive fluency: outputs get more convincing without any new kind of underlying capacity. A second dispute concerns ontology. When an analyzer studies simple cases built from a few objects, relations, and properties, the resulting compositional structure may reflect the world, or it may reflect the analyzer’s own habits of carving things up. A third dispute asks whether repeated filtering can ever generate truly new explanatory primitives, or only reorder inherited primitives into more effective presentations.

Second, there are two concrete scenes: vengeful crows presume the photos captured their ankle; designers completed a gassed shelter while hiding their crushes.

Treating all of this as one world, the concrete scenes become tests for the theoretical dispute. The analyzer is presented with entities and relations: crows, photos, an ankle; designers, a shelter, crushes; presuming, capturing, completing, hiding; properties like vengeful and gassed. This fits the journal’s style of sparse compositional examples. But because it is already admitted that such ontology may be imposed, not discovered, there is no guarantee that “crow,” “photo,” “ankle,” “designer,” and “shelter” are the world’s basic explanatory units rather than the analyzer’s preferred bookkeeping.

That uncertainty matters immediately. “Their ankle” underdetermines reference: it could be the crows’ collective ankle, someone else’s ankle associated with them, or an imposed grammatical convenience. “Gassed shelter” also underdetermines whether the shelter contains gas, was attacked with gas, or is simply labeled that way by the analyzer. “While hiding their crushes” introduces another ambiguity: the designers conceal their romantic attachments, or conceal persons on whom they have crushes. The scene is representable compositionally, but the representation does not settle whether its primitives are real or projected.

Now apply the filtering question. Repeated analysis may refine these scenes into cleaner schemas, but from the premises alone it cannot be concluded that this process creates any genuinely new primitive. It may only rearrange inherited categories—agent, object, relation, property, possession, concealment—into ever more persuasive explanations. If those explanations feel like sudden insight, the first premise says not to infer a phase transition; the effect may be only gradually improved fluency, including fluency about ambiguous cases.

So the collision implies a cautionary world: the more elegantly one explains why crows think photos captured their ankle, or how designers finished a gassed shelter while concealing crushes, the less entitled one is to claim that a threshold of novelty has been crossed or that new primitives have been born. One may instead be watching an analyzer become increasingly adept at imposing a compositional order on sentences that tolerate multiple incompatible decompositions.
